history.label: Origins
history.text: According to Eritrean Tigrinya oral traditional history, there were four clans living in the Asmera area on the Kebessa Plateau: the Gheza Gurtom, the Gheza Shelele, the Gheza Serenser and Gheza Asmae.

The first mention of Asmara comes from a Latin itinerary during the reign of Emperor Dawit I (1382–1411). Italian Asmara Asmara, a small village in the nineteenth century, started to grow quickly when it.
Governor Ferdinando Martini made it the capital city of Italian Eritrea in 1897.

history.label: 20th century
history.text: In the early 20th century, the Eritrean Railway was built to the coast, passing through the town of Ghinda, under the direction of Carlo Cavanna.
In both 1913 and 1915 the city suffered only slight damage in large earthquakes.

history.label: 1930s
history.text: By the late 1930s, Asmara was called Piccola Roma (Little Rome).
The city was home to the US Army's Kagnew Station installation from 1943 until 1977.
The Eritrean War of Independence began in 1961 and ended in 1991, resulting in the independence of Eritrea.

history.label: Growth
history.text: These villages were frequently attacked by clans from the lowlands, until the women of each clan decided that to preserve peace the four clans must unite.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The Kingdom of Italy invested in the industrial development of Asmara (and surrounding areas of Eritrea), but the beginning of World War II brought this to a halt.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: During the Federation, Asmara was no longer the capital city.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: After independence, Asmara again became the capital of Eritrea.
